title: Gravitational Waves Reveal the Cosmic Missing Link
oneliner: A neural network instantly decodes gravitational waves to reveal hidden objects in the mass gap between neutron stars and black holes.
description: Merging black holes and neutron stars send out gravitational waves. A neural network trained on these signals can instantly tell if a gap object—the missing link between neutron stars and black holes—is involved. Quick classification directs telescopes to the fleeting light of kilonovae, where heavy elements are born.
text:
When black holes or neutron stars merge, they strum space, broadcasting [tag:gravitational_waves]gravitational waves[/tag]—a chirp predicted by [scientist:Albert Einstein]Einstein[/scientist]. Detectors like LIGO capture this cosmic ringtone, its pitch encoding the masses. Yet, there's a silent interval in the cosmic scale: objects from 2 to 5 Suns are almost never heard. This [tag:neutron_star]lower mass gap[/tag] lies between the heaviest [tag:neutron_star]neutron stars[/tag] and lightest [tag:black_hole]black holes[/tag].

GWSkyNet-MassGap, a neural network, listens to the [tag:gravitational_waves]gravitational wave[/tag] chirp and judges if a merger includes a neutron star or a gap object—like naming instruments in an orchestra. It's accurate for loud signals but can mishear the mass split in faint ones. If neutron stars collide, telescopes catch a kilonova forging gold; black hole mergers stay dark. Sifting [tag:gravitational_waves]gravitational wave[/tag] catalogs, it spotlights rare candidates. The gap may hide surprises—perhaps quark-matter ghosts that are neither star nor hole.
fun_fact: A sugar-cube-sized lump of [tag:neutron_star]neutron star[/tag] matter would outweigh Mount Everest, making it the universe's densest known material outside a black hole.
mini: A 'missing link' between neutron stars and black holes has long puzzled astronomers. Now, a neural network can spot these cosmic middleweights in seconds from gravitational waves. Quick detection means telescopes might catch the flash of light from a rare neutron star merger, unlocking secrets of how heavy elements like gold are made.
